The team signed Kyler Murray and is actively exploring quarterback options. McCarthy is expected to face significant competition for the starting job.

items.headline: Tight end restructures contract to remain with Vikings; $5M cap savings
items.summary: Minnesota Vikings TE T.J. Hockenson restructured his contract for 2026, reducing his cap hit from $21.2M to ~$16M. The 28-year-old caught 51 passes for 438 yards and 3 TDs in 15 games during 2025, his lowest production since joining the team in 2022. Better QB play is expected to boost his 2026 numbers.

items.headline: DUI plea to wet reckless; three-game NFL suspension served in 2025
items.summary: Minnesota Vikings WR Jordan Addison was arrested near LAX on July 12, 2024, after being found asleep at the wheel. He pleaded no contest to wet reckless driving in July 2025, receiving a $390 fine and 12-month probation. Addison served a three-game NFL suspension for violating the substance abuse policy during the 2025 season.

items.headline: 31-year-old RB restructures deal, cuts salary in half to stay in Minnesota
items.summary: Minnesota Vikings RB Aaron Jones agreed to a revised one-year contract for 2026 worth $5.6M with $5M guaranteed, down from his original $9M base salary. The 31-year-old will remain in purple for a third season, serving as the complementary back alongside Jordan Mason.

items.headline: Mother's miracle: adopted into family story with grandmother Gerri
items.summary: Bijan Robinson was named after an Iranian fashion designer meaning 'hero,' and his grandmother Gerri performed a miraculous moment when baby Bijan stopped breathing moments after birth—she placed two fingers on his forehead and he took his first breath. Robinson was raised by his grandparents Gerri and Cleo Robinson after his mother LaMore returned from college to have him at Mesa Community College in 2002. His grandfather Cleo was a Pac-12 official for 30 years.

items.headline: Partial ACL tear in Week 11; targeting Week 1 return in 2026
items.summary: Michael Penix Jr. suffered a partially torn ACL in his left knee during a Week 11 game against the Carolina Panthers on November 16, 2025. He had surgery the week of November 24 and is targeting a Week 1 return for the 2026 season, putting him on a 9-month recovery timeline. Two months post-surgery, Penix reported he's already progressed to doing squats. He has prior experience recovering from two right ACL tears at Indiana in 2018 and 2020.

items.headline: Released by Falcons on March 11, end of $180 million era
items.summary: The Atlanta Falcons released Kirk Cousins on March 11, 2026, the first day of the new league year. Cousins had signed a 4-year, $180 million deal in 2024 but struggled in Atlanta, throwing a league-leading 16 interceptions in 2024. GM Ian Cunningham restructured his deal to reduce his 2026 cap hit from $35M to $2.1M before releasing him out of respect after what Cousins accomplished in his career.

items.headline: Falcons lock in WR1 with fifth-year option; extension talks ongoing
items.summary: The Atlanta Falcons picked up Drake London's fifth-year option, keeping him under contract through the 2026 season. London is projected as the team's WR1 going forward. GM Ian Cunningham says London's future and a potential long-term extension are top of mind, with extension talks underway. In 2025, London posted 60 receptions for 810 yards and 6 touchdowns in 9 games.

items.headline: Brother Avieon drafted by Falcons in 2026; reunited as teammates
items.summary: The Atlanta Falcons selected cornerback Avieon Terrell, the younger brother of star CB A.J. Terrell, with the No. 48 overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft. This marks the first time the brothers are teammates in the NFL. A.J. was also honored as the Falcons' club winner for the Walter Payton Man of the Year Award, a testament to his community impact in Atlanta.

items.headline: Franchise tagged for $15.05M; prove-it year before extension talks
items.summary: The Falcons placed the franchise tag on Kyle Pitts on February 24, 2026, guaranteeing him $15.05 million for 2026. New coach Kevin Stefanski, who has a history of featuring tight ends, favors preventing Pitts from leaving via the tag rather than losing him in free agency. Pitts had a career-high 928 receiving yards and 5 touchdowns in 2025, earning his first All-Pro selection. The team has until July 15 to negotiate a long-term deal.

items.headline: Panthers pick up fifth-year option after career season and playoff run
items.summary: The Carolina Panthers officially picked up the fifth-year option for quarterback Bryce Young on April 29, 2026, keeping him through 2027. Young had a career season in 2025, setting new highs in passing yards (3,011), touchdowns (23), completion percentage (63.6%), and passer rating (87.8). He led the Panthers to their first playoff appearance since 2017 and first division title since 2015.

items.headline: Emerged as reliable No. 2 WR for Bryce Young down the stretch
items.summary: Jalen Coker signed his exclusive rights free agent tender to stay in Carolina for 2026. After missing the first six games with a quad injury, he logged 33 receptions for 394 yards and 3 touchdowns in 11 games. He had a breakout playoff performance with 9 catches for 134 yards and a go-ahead TD against the Rams. Coker's emergence alongside OROY Tetairoa McMillan gives Carolina hope for 2026.

items.headline: Extended in 2024; fighting back from bench role in 2025
items.summary: Chuba Hubbard signed a 4-year, $33.2 million extension with the Panthers on November 7, 2024, with $15M guaranteed. He began the 2025 season as the starting RB but suffered a calf injury in Week 4 and lost carries to Rico Dowdle, who eventually took over as the lead back. Hubbard regained a more significant role late in the season and will likely be the top RB in 2026 with Dowdle set to become a free agent.

items.headline: Signed $100M extension; highest-paid defensive back in the league
items.summary: The Carolina Panthers signed cornerback Jaycee Horn to a 4-year, $100 million contract extension, making him the highest-paid defensive back in the league. The extension keeps him through 2029. Horn was named to the 2026 Pro Bowl Games for the second consecutive year and tied for second in the NFL with 5 interceptions in 2025, a career high that matches his total from his first four seasons.

items.headline: Agreed to 4-year, $96M extension; elite defensive tackle
items.summary: Defensive tackle Derrick Brown agreed to a 4-year, $96 million contract extension with the Panthers with $63 million guaranteed. Brown has been a standout performer in 2025, playing what scouts describe as dominant football. He expressed disappointment at not making the Pro Bowl despite impressive statistics, though his teammates were likewise surprised by the snub.

items.headline: Retired May 2025 due to shoulder injury; flirting with comeback
items.summary: Derek Carr announced his retirement on May 10, 2025, citing longstanding pain in his right shoulder from a labral tear and degenerative rotator cuff damage. However, Carr has flirted with unretiring for the 2026 season. Sources indicate he is 'very serious' about returning to the NFL, though the Saints plan to trade him if he unretires. No teams have called the Saints about acquiring him as of March 2026.

items.headline: Career-high 100 catches, All-Pro honors; extension talks ongoing
items.summary: Chris Olave had a breakout 2025 season with 100 receptions (career-high) for 1,163 yards and 9 touchdowns, earning Associated Press second-team All-Pro honors. He rebounded after missing 9 games in 2024 due to multiple concussions. The Saints exercised his fifth-year option for 2026 at $15.4 million. Extension talks are ongoing but stalled over price; Olave seeks $35+ million annually while the Saints are in the $30M range.

items.headline: Contract restructured in March 2026; future with Saints uncertain
items.summary: Alvin Kamara restructured his contract in March 2026, lowering his cap hit for the season by roughly $8 million. The Saints have not made a clear decision on his future, with reports indicating the team wants to address his contract further through a potential pay cut or roster cut. Kamara isn't in line to lead the Saints backfield in 2026 as Travis Etienne agreed to a 4-year deal with New Orleans. Kamara has stated he doesn't want to play anywhere outside New Orleans.

items.headline: Free agent after 15 seasons; Saints uncertain about reunion
items.summary: Cameron Jordan remains a free agent and is unclear about a return to New Orleans after 15 seasons. The veteran edge rusher told reporters the situation 'must make sense for both parties.' The Saints added two veteran pass rushers (Tyree Wilson via trade and Anfernee Jennings) and didn't draft an edge rusher, signaling they want to evaluate the pass rush without Jordan. The 36-year-old is the Saints' all-time franchise sack leader and a three-time All-Pro selection.

items.headline: Traded to 49ers on March 12, 2026; ends 12-year Buccaneers era
items.summary: Mike Evans signed a 3-year deal worth up to $42.4 million with the San Francisco 49ers on March 12, 2026, ending his 12-year tenure with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers. Evans's 11-year streak of 1,000-yard receiving seasons ended in an injury-plagued 2025 in which he totaled 30 catches for 368 yards and 3 scores in 8 appearances, dealing with hamstring and broken clavicle injuries.

items.headline: $66M extension with Bowles predicting 1,000-yard season ahead
items.summary: Chris Godwin signed a 3-year, $66 million extension with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers with an average annual salary of $22 million. He carries a 2026 cap hit of $33.683 million, highest among NFL wideouts. Godwin struggled in 2025 after returning from a 2024 ankle injury, missing time with a fibula injury and posting career-lows in receiving stats. Head coach Todd Bowles predicts Godwin could return to 1,000 receiving yards in 2026, returning to a full-time slot role.

items.headline: Extension talks ongoing; owner Glazer willing to pay $50M+ annually
items.summary: Baker Mayfield is in his final year of his 3-year, $100 million contract with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers, currently earning just over $33 million per season (19th among QBs). GM Jason Licht stated 'Baker is at the forefront of our mind at all times.' Owner Joel Glazer indicated he's willing to pay what it takes to extend Mayfield beyond 2026. Mayfield threw 40+ touchdowns in 2024 but had an MVP-caliber start to 2025 before a late-season skid.

items.headline: 23-year-old back expects no residual issues; ready for training camp
items.summary: Bucky Irving missed a large portion of the 2025 regular season with foot and shoulder injuries sustained in Week 4 against Philadelphia. GM Jason Licht told reporters at the NFL Scouting Combine that Irving will 'definitely' be ready for training camp in 2026 with no residual issues expected. Irving returned in Week 13 and gained 588 yards on 173 carries with 1 touchdown. With Rachaad White hitting free agency, Irving will have expanded importance as a three-down back in 2026.

items.headline: Highest-paid OT in NFL history; named to 2026 Pro Bowl
items.summary: On August 1, 2024, Tristan Wirfs and the Buccaneers agreed to a 5-year, $140.63 million contract extension, making him the highest-paid offensive lineman in NFL history. Wirfs was named to the 2026 Pro Bowl roster, earning his fifth Pro Bowl invitation. He underwent arthroscopic knee surgery in July 2025 and made his season debut in Week 4 against Philadelphia. Wirfs became the first player in NFL history to be named first-team All-Pro at both right tackle and left tackle.

items.headline: Played full 17-game season; extension through 2026 at $22.2M cap
items.summary: Vita Vea signed a 4-year, $73 million contract extension with the Buccaneers on January 8, 2022, keeping him under contract through 2026 with a $22.2 million cap hit. In 2025, Vea recorded 34 tackles (16 solo) with 4.5 sacks, 2 defensed passes and 1 fumble recovery across all 17 regular-season games—the first time since 2019 he's played a full season. The Super Bowl LV champion was selected to his first Pro Bowl the following year.

items.headline: Cardinals contract bans baseball; $4.6M bonus at stake
items.summary: Kyler Murray's NFL contract includes a broad no-baseball clause that triggers default on guaranteed money if he pursues any involvement with organized baseball. He previously forfeited a $4.6 million signing bonus from the Oakland Athletics in 2019 when he chose the NFL Draft, and Cardinals now control his baseball future.

sources.name: NBC Sports - Kyler Murray's contract has baseball provision
MLB.com - A's open to Murray reunion

items.headline: Safety lands $54M extension after requesting trade or new deal
items.summary: After two years of negotiating for improved terms, Budda Baker finally secured a 3-year extension with the Cardinals worth up to $54 million with $30 million guaranteed. Baker was voted team captain for the fifth consecutive year in 2024 and had become the backbone of Arizona's defense both on and off field.

sources.name: ESPN - Arizona Cardinals safety Budda Baker lands 3-year extension

items.headline: Rookie CB tears Achilles late in season after strong 2024 play
items.summary: Cornerback Garrett Williams, a 2023 third-round draft pick, became one of the league's best slot corners in 2024 with 58 tackles and two interceptions across 16 games. He suffered a torn Achilles injury late in the season after recovering from a knee injury in Week 2.

sources.name: Arizona Sports - Cardinals' Garrett Williams out for year with torn Achilles

items.headline: Wife Kelly pauses viral podcast to focus on mental health
items.summary: Kelly Stafford, Matthew's wife and host of 'The Morning After' podcast, announced in October 2025 that she was taking an indefinite break from podcasting. The podcast launched in September 2021 and became a viral platform where Kelly discussed marriage, parenting, and life in the NFL spotlight.

sources.name: E! Online - Kelly Stafford podcast break for mental health
Just Jared - Matthew Stafford's wife Kelly halting podcast

items.headline: Running back credits mother's sacrifice for fueling NFL dream
items.summary: Kyren Williams told his mother Taryn at age five he would become an NFL player—a prophecy she witnessed come true. His mom attended his games, including catching a touchdown pass he tossed to her in the stands after he scored to clinch a playoff bid for the Rams.

sources.name: ESPN - How the Rams' Kyren Williams was buoyed by his mom

items.headline: RB named 2025 Walter Payton Man of the Year finalist
items.summary: Kyren Williams earned recognition as the 2025 Walter Payton Man of the Year club winner for his off-field community work, including helping Palisades fire victims and other charitable initiatives.

sources.name: Bollywood Shaadis - Kyren Williams profile with charity work

items.headline: Edge rusher wins 2024 AP Defensive Rookie of the Year
items.summary: Jared Verse, a 2024 first-round draft pick from Florida State, won both the AP and PFWA Defensive Rookie of the Year awards in his first season with the Rams. He recorded 7.5 sacks and was second in the league in quarterback pressures with 100.

sources.name: ESPN - Jared Verse elite trash talker defensive stalwart

items.headline: WR benched as healthy scratch in NFC Championship despite $10M deal
items.summary: Despite signing a one-year, $10 million contract in March 2025, Tutu Atwell became a healthy inactive for the Rams' NFC Championship game against Seattle. The team shifted to 13 personnel with three tight ends, and Atwell's role diminished after Week 7, leaving the Rams front office with buyer's remorse.

sources.name: SI.com - Rams explain what went wrong with Tutu Atwell

items.headline: WR took contract negotiations 'personal' with 49ers organization
items.summary: Brandon Aiyuk signed a 4-year, $120 million extension with the 49ers in August 2024 after initially rejecting their offer. He stated he was taking negotiations personally because the team didn't see him as the player he envisions himself to be, and prioritized 'taking care of my family.'

sources.name: Yahoo Sports - Aiyuk taking 49ers contract negotiations personal

items.headline: DE fined $11k for MAGA hat worn during postgame interview
items.summary: On October 27, 2024, Nick Bosa crashed a postgame TV interview wearing a MAGA hat following the 49ers' win over Dallas. The NFL fined him $11,255 for violating uniform and equipment rules. Bosa said the fine was 'well worth it' when asked.

sources.name: NFL.com - Niners Nick Bosa fined for MAGA hat
CNN - 49ers lineman Nick Bosa fined for crashing interview with MAGA hat

items.headline: Cornerback's daughter Amani Joy passes away at 23 months
items.summary: 49ers cornerback Charvarius Ward's daughter Amani Joy passed away on October 28, 2024, at 23 months old after battling Down syndrome and heart issues since birth. In his Instagram announcement, Ward called Amani 'the best blessing' and credited her with teaching him patience, trust, and true strength.

sources.name: Today.com - 49ers Player Charvarius Ward's Daughter Amani Died At 1 Year Old
NBC Sports Bay Area - Charvarius Ward announces death of daughter Amani Joy

items.headline: WR inks record-breaking $168.6M extension, highest-paid pass catcher
items.summary: Jaxon Smith-Njigba signed a 4-year, $168.6 million extension with the Seahawks making him the highest-paid wide receiver in NFL history at $42.15M per year. The deal includes $120 million guaranteed and keeps him through 2031.

sources.name: NFL.com - Seahawks signing WR Jaxon Smith-Njigba to record extension
Sky Sports - Seahawks' Smith-Njigba becomes highest paid WR

items.headline: All-Pro OT advocates for diabetes awareness after grandfather's death
items.summary: Trent Williams, considered one of the greatest offensive tackles of all time with 12 Pro Bowls, became an American Diabetes Association ambassador in 2014 after losing his paternal grandfather John L. Hawkins to Type 2 diabetes in his childhood.

items.headline: LB hosts 'Real Ones' podcast featuring NFL stars and celebrities
items.summary: 49ers linebacker Fred Warner hosts 'Real Ones: The League' podcast where he interviews notable figures from sports and entertainment. He also partners with Bay Area law firm Venardi Zurada to host community Thanksgiving Food Drives.

sources.name: San Francisco 49ers - Mike Evans Joins Fred Warner on Real Ones

items.headline: Four-time All-Star tore meniscus playing overseas
items.summary: Brionna Jones, a four-time WNBA All-Star, suffered a torn meniscus in February 2025 while playing for USK Praha in Europe. She underwent surgery at Atlanta's Emory Hospital and was expected to recover by the May 8 WNBA season opener, though her availability remained uncertain based on rehab progress.

items.headline: Left Connecticut Sun after eight years to join Atlanta
items.summary: After eight years with the Connecticut Sun (2017-2024), Brionna Jones signed with the Atlanta Dream in free agency in January 2025. In her first season with Atlanta, she earned her fourth All-Star selection, averaging 12.8 points and 7.3 rebounds while shooting 52.7 percent from the field.

items.headline: Two-time WNBA champion from Seattle Storm legacy
items.summary: Jordin Canada won WNBA championships with the Seattle Storm in 2018 and 2020, serving as a key backcourt player and multiple-time All-Defensive honoree. After stints with the Las Vegas Aces and Los Angeles Sparks, she returned to Atlanta as a veteran floor general leading the Dream's offense.

items.headline: UCLA legend set Pac-12 record with 831 assists
items.summary: Jordin Canada became the UCLA women's basketball program and Pac-12 Conference leader with 831 career assists, and was one of four Bruins to record 100+ assists in a season four times. She also became the first UCLA women's player to earn Pac-12 Defensive Player of the Year from the league coaches.

items.headline: Parents shaped her from Newark with military and education roots
items.summary: Brittney Sykes was born in Newark, New Jersey, to parents Michael and Regina Sykes. Her father, Michael, is a U.S. Army veteran who worked for the Newark Police Department and the State of New Jersey. Her mother, Regina Sherrod Sykes, is an operations coordinator at Great Oaks Legacy Charter School and emphasized education and resilience.

items.headline: Signed with expansion Toronto Tempo in 2026 free agency
items.summary: Brittney Sykes, a 2025 WNBA All-Star and accomplished two-way guard, signed a multi-year contract with the new Toronto Tempo franchise in 2026 free agency. She joined fellow All-Star Marina Mabrey as one of the expansion team's top acquisitions.

items.headline: 2025 WNBA All-Star named as face of Toronto Tempo franchise
items.summary: Brittney Sykes, a 2025 WNBA All-Star, signed a multi-year deal with the Toronto Tempo on April 11, 2026, as one of the franchise's cornerstone players. She is widely recognized for her elite defensive abilities and postseason experience.

items.headline: Elite two-way guard with toughest defensive assignments in league
items.summary: Brittney Sykes is widely recognized as one of the most respected perimeter defenders in the WNBA. In her ninth WNBA season with the Tempo, she owns career averages of 12.3 points, 4.0 rebounds, and 2.9 assists per game.

items.headline: Selected by Toronto Tempo in 2026 expansion draft from Liberty
items.summary: Nyara Sabally was selected by the new Toronto Tempo in the 2026 WNBA Expansion Draft on April 3, 2026, ending her tenure with the New York Liberty. The German-American center had won a WNBA championship with Liberty in 2024 and was a key contributor in their Finals run.

items.headline: Won 2024 WNBA Championship with New York Liberty
items.summary: Nyara Sabally helped the New York Liberty win their first WNBA championship in franchise history in 2024. In Game 5 of the WNBA Finals, she scored 13 points and grabbed 7 rebounds in 17 minutes off the bench.

items.headline: Coach Sandy Brondello expects bigger role in Toronto than New York
items.summary: Toronto Tempo coach Sandy Brondello, who previously coached Nyara Sabally with the New York Liberty, is excited for the center to have an expanded role in Toronto than she had in New York. Brondello noted that Sabally is apparently the healthiest she's been since entering the WNBA.

items.headline: Co-won 2025 Defensive Player of Year with A'ja Wilson
items.summary: Alanna Smith and A'ja Wilson made history as the first co-winners of the WNBA Defensive Player of the Year award in 2025, each receiving 29 of 72 media votes. Smith, with the Minnesota Lynx, ranked second in combined steals and blocks (135) while helping Minnesota post the league's best defensive rating of 97.5.

items.headline: Australian hoops royalty from Melbourne with NBL heritage
items.summary: Alanna Smith comes from a basketball family in Melbourne, Australia. Her father Darren Smith played professionally in the NBL for 12 years and now serves as Chief Operating Officer of the Tasmania JackJumpers. Her uncle Jason Smith represented the Australian Boomers at the 2000 and 2004 Olympics, and her aunt Jo Straatsma played professionally in the WNBL.

items.headline: Engaged to real estate broker N'Shya Seigle
items.summary: Courtney Williams announced her engagement to N'Shya Seigle, a real estate broker based in Charlotte, on November 7, 2024. In a romantic proposal, Williams had N'Shya blindfolded and led her to a grassy clearing with giant light-up letters spelling 'WILL YOU MARRY ME,' with a saxophonist playing softly. The couple has been together since 2022.

items.headline: Co-hosts podcast with father Don Williams spotlighting their bond
items.summary: In 2025, Courtney Williams and her father, Don Williams, premiered their co-hosted podcast 'Courtney's Daddy & Her,' celebrating their deep family connection. Don is famous in WNBA circles for his enthusiastic courtside support and was her primary trainer growing up.

items.headline: Openly gay Lynx star reflects on family acceptance and pride
items.summary: Courtney Williams is an openly gay athlete and active supporter of LGBTQ+ visibility in professional sports. She credits her father Don's unwavering love and support after coming out with helping her thrive both on and off the court.

items.headline: Traded to Connecticut Sun from Dallas Wings
items.summary: Diamond Miller was traded from the Dallas Wings to the Connecticut Sun on April 9, 2026, in exchange for center Rayah Marshall. This marked Miller's third team in three years after being drafted second overall by Minnesota Lynx in 2023. The Sun hoped to give the young forward more playing time to develop her potential.

items.headline: Three-time Olympic gold medalist for Team USA basketball
items.summary: Tina Charles has represented the United States on the international stage, winning Olympic gold medals in 2012, 2016, and 2020, plus three FIBA World Cup gold medals (2010, 2014, 2018) as a member of Team USA.

items.headline: WNBA all-time rebounds leader with humanitarian ambassador work
items.summary: Tina Charles became the league's all-time rebounds leader (4,014 as of 2024). Beyond basketball, she serves as OmniPeace's sports ambassador and donated $32,000 to build a school in Mali.

items.headline: Hard-launched romance with teammate Saniya Rivers on Valentine's Day 2026
items.summary: After months of speculation, Marina Mabrey and Connecticut Sun teammate Saniya Rivers released intimate photos on Valentine's Day 2026, with both posting red heart emoji captions. Rivers, 22, and Mabrey, 28, have a close bond after bonding over binge-watching together.

items.headline: Fills maternal void for rookie teammate Saniya Rivers after mother's death
items.summary: Saniya Rivers credits Marina Mabrey for emotional support following her mother's passing. Rivers says Mabrey 'has a big heart, is a sensitive person' and they 'cry together' and 'share that emotional bond' off the court.

items.headline: Signs with Toronto Tempo on max deal as franchise cornerstones
items.summary: Marina Mabrey agreed to a two-year deal at approximately the WNBA max in Year 1 with the Toronto Tempo. She and Brittney Sykes formed what was believed to be the first million-dollar backcourt pairing under the WNBA's new collective bargaining agreement.

items.headline: Connecticut Sun's second leading scorer before expansion departure
items.summary: Before signing with the Toronto Tempo, Marina Mabrey was the Connecticut Sun's second leading scorer, averaging 14.4 points, 4.2 rebounds, and 4.0 assists per game during the 2025 season. She was a key piece of the Sun's roster after being traded there in July 2024.

items.headline: Clinton, Maryland native became ACC all-time assist leader at Notre Dame
items.summary: Lindsay Allen was born in Clinton, Maryland to parents Terrell Allen and Lorna Wilson-Allen. Her early exposure to sports through her older brother helped her discover basketball at age seven, leading to four ACC titles and the distinction of Notre Dame's all-time assist leader.

items.headline: Reached 600 career assists in first Sun game vs. Washington Mystics
items.summary: In the Connecticut Sun's season opener on May 18, 2025 against the Washington Mystics, Lindsay Allen dished out her 600th career assist, becoming the 91st player in WNBA history to reach that milestone.

items.headline: Named SEC Female Athlete of the Year while at South Carolina
items.summary: In June 2020, Tyasha Harris was named the Southeastern Conference Female Athlete of the Year while at the University of South Carolina, where she ranked 10th all-time in SEC assists with 705 career dimes.

items.headline: Left knee injury ended 2025 season after just five games with Wings
items.summary: After being traded from Connecticut to Dallas, Tyasha Harris suffered an early-season knee injury in 2025. On June 10, 2025, the Wings announced she had undergone left knee surgery and would miss the remainder of the season.

items.headline: Engaged to Drexel assistant coach Steve Perretta, met at Villanova
items.summary: Maddy Siegrist is engaged to Stephen Perretta, assistant coach and Director of Basketball Operations for Drexel women's basketball. The two met at Villanova where Perretta helped during team practices, and married at Villanova Chapel on October 31.

items.headline: Villanova all-time leading scorer with 2,896 career points
items.summary: As a four-year starter at Villanova, Maddy Siegrist graduated as the program's all-time leading scorer (2,896 points) and all-time leading rebounder (1,102 boards). As a senior, she averaged 29 points and 9.1 rebounds per game and scored a career-high 50 points against Seton Hall on February 11, 2023.

items.headline: German basketball star committed to represent Germany at EuroBasket 2025
items.summary: Luisa Geiselsöder, born February 10, 2000, is a German professional basketball player. In 2025, she committed to represent Germany at EuroBasket 2025 while also playing for the Dallas Wings.

items.headline: Finally made WNBA debut five years after being drafted by Dallas in 2020
items.summary: Although drafted 21st overall by the Dallas Wings in 2020, Luisa Geiselsöder didn't sign with the WNBA until February 2, 2025, due to injuries. She made her WNBA debut on May 27, 2025, and appeared in 28 games, averaging 6.9 points and 4.8 rebounds.

items.headline: Born in Egypt, moved to Finland as two-year-old refugee, first Finnish WNBA player
items.summary: Awak Kuier was born in Cairo, Egypt to South Sudanese parents. Her family moved to Finland when she was two years old, settling first in Kotka before relocating to Uusikaupunki. She holds dual Finnish-Egyptian citizenship and became the first Finnish player to play in the WNBA in May 2021.

items.headline: Eighth woman in WNBA history to successfully dunk a basketball
items.summary: On June 10, 2022, in a game against the Seattle Storm, Awak Kuier became only the eighth woman in WNBA history to dunk a basketball. At 6-foot-6, the Finnish center joined an elite group of dunking players in the league.

items.headline: Dallas Wings reacquire Awak Kuier amid immigration paperwork delays
items.summary: The Dallas Wings brought back center Awak Kuier in April 2026 after she developed into a EuroLeague star playing for Galatasaray in Turkey during 2025-26. However, Kuier was awaiting an immigration appointment in Finland with no set return date to training camp. The Wings conducted remote nightly sessions to keep her prepared.

items.headline: Identical twin sister Lacie played college basketball together at Stanford
items.summary: Lexie Hull has an identical twin sister, Lacie, who was her teammate throughout high school and college. The twins won multiple championships at Central Valley High School in Spokane, Washington, then won an NCAA championship together at Stanford in 2021. After college, Lacie pursued a different path, becoming a project manager at eBay in Austin, Texas.

items.headline: Father played Division III basketball at Whitworth, grandfather at Western Washington
items.summary: Basketball runs deep in the Hull family. Lexie's father, Jason, played college basketball for Whitworth University where he earned NCAA Division III All-America honors. Her grandfather, John, played at Western Washington. This multi-generational basketball heritage shaped Lexie's path to becoming a Stanford star and Fever draft pick.

items.headline: Identical twin Lacie chose corporate path at eBay while Lexie plays WNBA
items.summary: Lexie Hull is an identical twin; her sister Lacie was her basketball teammate in high school and college at Stanford. When Lexie was drafted by Fever, Lacie started a career at eBay in Austin, Texas. The twins stay connected via FaceTime, calls, texts, Snapchat, Instagram DMs, and TikTok.

items.headline: Adult baptism in April 2026 marked personal faith recommitment after signing with Fever
items.summary: In April 2026, Sophie Cunningham was baptized for the second time at Christ's Church of the Valley in Phoenix, Arizona. Although baptized as a child, she felt compelled to recommit on her own terms, calling it 'one of the better things' she's done in her life. She shared that she's 'always been a faithful person' but wanted to make the choice herself.

items.headline: Devout Christian athlete frequently shares faith messages on social media
items.summary: Sophie Cunningham makes no secret of her deep Christian faith, frequently posting faith-related messages on social media and attributing her basketball talent as a gift from God. Her public devotion and spiritual life are central to her identity as both an athlete and person.

items.headline: Christian faith and matching wave tattoo with sister Lindsey from Bible verse
items.summary: Cunningham was born and raised in Columbia, Missouri on her family's farm. She shares a special Christian faith bond with her sister Lindsey through a matching wave tattoo (three peaks) inspired by a Bible verse. Her parents, Jim and Paula, were both student athletes at University of Missouri.

items.headline: Invites Indiana Fever teammates to her family farm for team bonding
items.summary: Cunningham has a special connection to Bea, a horse on her family farm that has been with the family since before Sophie was born. She invites her Fever teammates to visit the family farm, integrating her personal roots with her basketball career.

items.headline: Traded mid-season from Dallas Wings to Las Vegas Aces for 2027 first-round pick
items.summary: During the 2025 WNBA season, NaLyssa Smith was traded from the Dallas Wings to the Las Vegas Aces in exchange for a 2027 first-round draft pick. Smith said she 'never seen this coming' but was excited to join A'ja Wilson and contend for a championship with Las Vegas.

items.headline: Won WNBA championship with Las Vegas Aces in 2025 after mid-season trade
items.summary: After joining the Aces mid-season in 2025, NaLyssa Smith thrived as a key contributor, averaging 8.2 points and 5.3 rebounds in 27 appearances. The Aces went on to win the 2025 WNBA championship, making Smith an instant champion with her new team.

items.headline: Retired 2023, won Sixth Player of the Year with Aces in 2024
items.summary: Tiffany Hayes announced her retirement in 2023 but made a surprising comeback, signing with the Las Vegas Aces in 2024 where she won the WNBA Sixth Player of the Year award. The 36-year-old veteran re-signed with the Golden State Valkyries for 2026 on a one-year deal worth $600,000.

items.headline: American-born guard made Olympic team for Azerbaijan
items.summary: Tiffany Hayes became an Azerbaijani citizen in 2015 and competed for the Azerbaijan women's 3x3 basketball team at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris. Born in Winter Haven, Florida, Hayes has represented both her native country and her adopted nation on the international stage.

items.headline: Named 2024 WNBA Sixth Woman of the Year before Valkyries
items.summary: Before signing with the Golden State Valkyries, Tiffany Hayes was named the 2024 WNBA Sixth Woman of the Year while playing for the Las Vegas Aces. The veteran guard of 13 years brought this honor with her when she signed a one-year, $600,000 contract with Golden State on February 6, 2025.

items.headline: Engaged to basketball player David DiLeo playing overseas in France
items.summary: Megan Gustafson announced her engagement to David DiLeo on July 18, 2025, during the WNBA All-Star weekend. DiLeo is an American professional basketball player for Le Mans Sarthe Basket in the French LNB Pro A, where he won the 2025 French League Cup. The couple shared an emotional send-off as DiLeo departed for France.

items.headline: Took social media jabs at pregnancy rumors amid concussion recovery
items.summary: Rickea Jackson trolled social media rumors claiming she was pregnant during a prolonged absence from the LA Sparks in 2025. Jackson had actually taken personal time to recover from a concussion earlier in the season. When rumors surfaced, she responded by posting a selfie in workout gear with a basketball against her stomach, poking fun at the speculation before returning to play.

items.headline: Traded from Sparks to Chicago Sky for Ariel Atkins in 2026
items.summary: Rickea Jackson was traded from the Los Angeles Sparks to the Chicago Sky on April 12, 2026, in exchange for veteran guard Ariel Atkins. Jackson, 25, averaged 14.7 points per game for the Sparks in 2025. She joined Chicago alongside free-agent signings Skylar Diggins and Azurá Stevens in a major Sky roster overhaul.

items.headline: Settled pregnancy discrimination lawsuit against Las Vegas Aces in December 2025
items.summary: Dearica Hamby filed a lawsuit against the Las Vegas Aces and WNBA in August 2024, claiming she was illegally traded because she was pregnant with her second child in 2023. The case alleged retaliation including loss of housing, unpaid school tuition, and team staff instructed not to communicate with her. Hamby and the Aces agreed to dismiss the lawsuit in December 2025 with prejudice, meaning it cannot be refiled.

items.headline: Hamby sues WNBA and Aces for pregnancy discrimination
items.summary: In September 2023, three-time All-Star Dearica Hamby filed a federal discrimination complaint against the WNBA and Las Vegas Aces, claiming the team traded her because she was pregnant and that the league did not adequately investigate.

items.headline: Finished second in Most Improved Player voting after career-high season
items.summary: Azura Stevens posted career-high averages of 12.8 points, 8.0 rebounds, and 2.1 assists in 2025 with the LA Sparks, finishing as runner-up in the WNBA Most Improved Player voting. She achieved a 60.4 Effective Field Goal Percentage, the top mark in the league among players with 200+ shot attempts.

items.headline: Returns to Chicago Sky on richest contract, three-year $3.15 million deal
items.summary: After three seasons with the LA Sparks, Azura Stevens re-signed with the Chicago Sky on April 11, 2026, on a three-year deal worth $3.15 million—the richest contract on the Sky roster. Stevens, a 6-foot-6 post, originally won the 2021 WNBA championship with Chicago before her stint in LA.

items.headline: Dual American-French citizen, plays for France and speaks out on WNBA salaries
items.summary: Gabby Williams holds dual American and French citizenship through her mother Thérèse, who is French. Williams plays for the French national team and represented France at the Paris Olympics, winning a silver medal. She has been vocal about WNBA salary concerns, stating the league's pay makes European opportunities more attractive despite her American roots.

items.headline: Left Seattle Storm, signed with Golden State Valkyries for Bay Area homecoming
items.summary: All-Star Gabby Williams signed a three-year, $3.7 million contract with the Golden State Valkyries on April 12, 2026, after four seasons with the Seattle Storm. Williams was born in Sparks, Nevada, and grew up with deep Bay Area ties. Her decision to return home marked a significant move in the expansion team's second season.

items.headline: Grew up in Nevada with siblings playing college and professional basketball
items.summary: Gabby Williams was raised by her parents Matthew (American) and Thérèse (French) in Sparks, Nevada. She has siblings who played college and professional basketball: older sister Kayla Williams played college ball at Nevada and professionally in Australia, and younger sister Jayda Noble competes for Cal as of 2025.

items.headline: First undrafted player in WNBA history to win All-Star Game MVP
items.summary: In 2019, Erica Wheeler became the first undrafted player in WNBA history to be named All-Star Game MVP while playing for the Indiana Fever. Wheeler scored 25 points with seven 3-pointers, including the game-winner with 22 seconds remaining, to lead Team Wilson to a 129-126 victory.

items.headline: Lost mother Melissa Cooper to cervical cancer, now advocates for screening awareness
items.summary: Erica Wheeler lost her mother, Melissa Cooper, to stage 4 cervical cancer on July 17, 2012, during her junior year at Rutgers University. Her mother, who ran a beauty salon and barber shop, was also Wheeler's best friend. Now 12 years later, Wheeler partners with Hologic to raise awareness about cervical cancer screening, particularly among Black women.

items.headline: Australian Opals teammate, won bronze at 2024 Olympics and All-Star honors
items.summary: Ezi Magbegor, a Melbourne native, represents Australia's Opals women's basketball team internationally. She earned a bronze medal with Australia at the 2024 Summer Olympics and was named to the 2025 WNBA All-Star team and All-Defensive First Team, her best season to date.

items.headline: Right foot injury from WNBA World Cup qualifiers sidelines season opener
items.summary: Magbegor suffered a right foot injury while playing for Australia in FIBA Basketball World Cup Qualifiers in early March 2026. The injury forced her to miss the Opals' final two qualifying games. Storm announced she would miss 6-8 weeks of the 2026 season, delaying her season debut.

items.headline: Married to Australian wife Kate, two sons Nash and Reef
items.summary: Phoenix Mercury guard Sami Whitcomb is married to Kate Malpass, a physiotherapist from Perth, Australia. The couple met as teammates on the Lynx in 2014 and married in the U.S. in 2017. They have two sons: Nash (born 2020) and Reef Augie (born 2023). Whitcomb became a naturalized Australian citizen in 2018.

items.headline: Once worked shearing chicken wings, fought back to WNBA
items.summary: After being discouraged from pursuing basketball, Natasha Mack took a job in a poultry plant shearing chicken wings and nearly gave up her dream. She fought back through junior college, led the nation in blocks at Oklahoma State (Naismith Defensive Player of the Year 2021), was drafted by Chicago in 2021, then played overseas for two years in Poland, New Zealand, and Turkey before signing with Phoenix Mercury in 2024.

items.headline: Traded from Washington to Chicago for No. 3 draft pick
items.summary: On February 23, 2025, the Washington Mystics traded two-time All-Star Ariel Atkins to the Chicago Sky for the No. 3 pick in the 2025 WNBA draft, plus 2027 draft selections. Atkins, a 2019 WNBA champion with Washington and 2020 Olympian, was the last remaining member of the Mystics' first championship squad.

items.headline: Founded Ariel Atkins Foundation to support teachers
items.summary: In 2023, Dallas-born Ariel Atkins launched the Ariel Atkins Foundation inspired by her sister Jessica, who worked as an educator. The foundation supports teachers and youth education, including teacher appreciation days, educational clinics, and programs teaching financial literacy and AI tools for lesson planning.

items.headline: Took leave from Sky to compete in FIBA AmeriCup with Brazil
items.summary: In June 2025, Kamilla Cardoso temporarily left the Chicago Sky to represent Brazil in the 2025 FIBA Women's AmeriCup tournament in Chile. She averaged 14.2 points, 10.7 rebounds, 2.2 assists, 2.2 blocks, and 1.2 steals per game, reminding fans why she was the No. 3 overall pick in the 2024 WNBA Draft.

items.headline: Split from NBA boyfriend Jalen Suggs after four years
items.summary: Hailey Van Lith and NBA star Jalen Suggs broke up in November 2025 after dating for four years since 2021. The couple shared a public kiss during Van Lith's WNBA draft night on April 14, 2025, but quietly ended the relationship before Suggs confirmed their split on a livestream with streamer N3on on November 8, 2025.

items.headline: Three major college transfers: Louisville to LSU to TCU
items.summary: Guard Hailey Van Lith played college basketball for three schools, each representing a major shift. She started at Louisville for three seasons, transferred to LSU for the 2023-24 championship run with Kim Mulkey, then moved to TCU for her final season where she averaged 17.7 PPG.

items.headline: Chicago Sky rookie Hailey Van Lith opens up about overcoming suicidal ideation
items.summary: Hailey Van Lith revealed she struggled with suicidal thoughts during college and was heavily medicated. She credited TCU coach Mark Campbell with transforming her life and helping her find comfort with herself. Van Lith said 'I'm comfortable with who I am. And I think when you have that, your mental health can even out a lot more.'

items.headline: Daughter of Nigerian immigrants from Igbo villages
items.summary: Michaela Onyenwere was born in Aurora, Colorado to Nigerian immigrant parents Peter and Edith Onyenwere from Igbo villages in southeastern Nigeria. Her father Peter, an elite sprinter, immigrated in 1985 to compete in track and field at the University of Missouri. Onyenwere has spoken proudly about her heritage, saying 'I am the daughter of Nigerian immigrants.'

items.headline: Selected to represent Nigeria in Olympic D'Tigress squad
items.summary: Michaela Onyenwere was selected to represent Nigeria in the D'Tigress Olympic basketball squad, honoring her heritage and immigrant parents' home country. She plays for the Chicago Sky and has been a vocal advocate for her Nigerian roots throughout her professional career.

items.headline: Traded from Mystics to Connecticut Sun mid-season
items.summary: On August 7, 2025, Aaliyah Edwards was traded from the Washington Mystics to the Connecticut Sun in exchange for Jacy Sheldon and the right to swap first-round picks. Edwards, a UConn product drafted 6th overall by Washington in 2024, returned to Connecticut where she played college basketball.

items.headline: Made All-Star Game as rookie, first teammate pair since 1999
items.summary: Kiki Iriafen was named a reserve for the 2025 All-Star Game in her rookie season, making her and teammate Sonia Citron the first rookie teammates selected as All-Stars since 1999 (only second time in league history). Iriafen averaged 13.3 points and 8.5 rebounds per game.

items.headline: All-Rookie honors with franchise record 16 double-doubles
items.summary: Kiki Iriafen earned All-Rookie Team honors in 2025, recording a franchise-record 16 double-doubles and the most rebounds (8.5 RPG) by a Mystics rookie in history. She was the 4th overall pick in the 2025 WNBA draft and reached 100 points and 80 rebounds in just eight games.

items.headline: Left Mystics to sign with Seattle Storm in free agency
items.summary: After playing the 2025 season with the Washington Mystics, veteran center Stefanie Dolson signed with the Seattle Storm as an unrestricted free agent in April 2026, ending her tenure with Washington.

items.headline: Wins AP Comeback Player of the Year after injury comeback
items.summary: Shakira Austin was named the 2025 Associated Press Comeback Player of the Year, the first Mystics player to earn this honor. After appearing in only 12 games in 2024 due to hip and ankle injuries, Austin appeared in 38 games in 2025, averaging career highs of 12.7 points, 6.4 rebounds, and 1.1 blocks.

items.headline: Mystics matched Toronto's $3.57M offer sheet to retain star
items.summary: After Shakira Austin signed an offer sheet with the Toronto Tempo for a three-year, $3.57 million deal, the Washington Mystics matched the offer on July 28, 2025, to keep their budding star. Austin, 25, was the 2025 AP Comeback Player of the Year.

items.headline: Brother Tidjane drafted 6th overall in 2024 NBA Draft
items.summary: Janelle Salaün's brother Tidjane was selected 6th overall in the 2024 NBA Draft by the Charlotte Hornets. The siblings are now playing professionally in the NBA and WNBA simultaneously. Both grew up in basketball families—their parents both played basketball professionally.

items.headline: Won silver medal representing France at 2024 Olympics
items.summary: Janelle Salaün was part of the French women's national basketball team that won a silver medal at the 2024 Summer Olympics in Paris. The French team finished as runners-up at the Olympics.

items.headline: Named to WNBA All-Rookie Team in inaugural Valkyries season
items.summary: Janelle Salaün made the WNBA All-Rookie Team in the Golden State Valkyries' inaugural 2025 season. She ranked 4th among WNBA rookies in points per game (11.3), 3rd in rebounds (5.1) and minutes (27.0), making her one of the league's top young performers.

items.headline: Seventh undrafted player ever selected as WNBA All-Star
items.summary: Kayla Thornton was named to the 2025 WNBA All-Star Game, becoming just the seventh undrafted player in WNBA history to receive the honor and the first since 2019. At age 33, she started all 22 of her appearances for Golden State, averaging 14.0 points and 7.0 rebounds.

items.headline: First All-Star in Golden State Valkyries franchise history
items.summary: Kayla Thornton became the Golden State Valkyries' first All-Star in franchise history when she was selected for the 2025 WNBA All-Star Game. She was also drafted by Caitlin Clark in the All-Star draft.

items.headline: Selected 9th by Golden State, traded to Portland in expansion draft
items.summary: French guard Carla Leite was drafted 9th overall by the Dallas Wings in the 2024 WNBA draft, then selected by the Golden State Valkyries in the 2024 expansion draft. On April 3, 2026, she was selected third overall by the Portland Fire in the inaugural 2026 WNBA expansion draft.

items.headline: Led Villeneuve d'Ascq to EuroCup championship with Finals MVP
items.summary: Before her 2026 WNBA expansion draft selection, Carla Leite led French club Villeneuve d'Ascq to a championship in the 2024-25 EuroCup. She earned Finals MVP honors with averages of 20.5 points, 6.0 assists, and 2.5 rebounds per game.

items.headline: Brother Rayan signs with Memphis Grizzlies on two-way deal
items.summary: Iliana Rupert's brother Rayan Rupert signed a two-way contract with the NBA's Memphis Grizzlies. Both siblings are now playing professional basketball at the highest levels—Iliana in the WNBA and Rayan in the NBA.

items.headline: Father Thierry was former power forward for French national team
items.summary: Iliana Rupert is the daughter of Thierry Rupert, a former power forward who played for the French national basketball team. Basketball runs in the Rupert family bloodline.

items.headline: Led Valkyries in 3-point shooting at 44.3%, ranked 2nd in WNBA
items.summary: Iliana Rupert emerged as one of the most efficient shooters in the WNBA during the 2025 season, leading the Valkyries with a 44.3 three-point shooting percentage that ranked second in the entire league. She also averaged career highs in points (9.3) and rebounds (3.9).

items.headline: Toronto Tempo's first-ever WNBA draft pick at sixth overall
items.summary: Kiki Rice was selected sixth overall in the 2026 WNBA Draft by the Toronto Tempo on April 13, 2026, becoming the franchise's first-ever pick. The UCLA guard won an NCAA championship with the Bruins in 2026.

items.headline: Received support from NBA boyfriend Cason Wallace at draft
items.summary: Kiki Rice had support from her NBA boyfriend Cason Wallace at the 2026 WNBA Draft night. Wallace attended the draft to show his support as Rice was selected by the Toronto Tempo.

items.headline: Led UCLA Bruins to 2026 NCAA Women's Basketball Championship
items.summary: Kiki Rice helped lead the UCLA Bruins to the 2026 NCAA Women's Basketball Championship. As a senior, she averaged 14.9 points, 5.9 rebounds, and 4.3 assists per game, and made third-team All-American honors.

items.headline: Selected 9th by Portland Fire in 2026 expansion draft
items.summary: Maya Caldwell, a 5-foot-11 wing guard, was selected ninth overall by the Portland Fire in the 2026 WNBA expansion draft on April 3, 2026. She came from the Atlanta Dream, where she had played the previous four WNBA seasons.

items.headline: Former University of Georgia basketball standout
items.summary: Maya Caldwell is a former standout basketball player from the University of Georgia who signed a training camp contract with the Portland Fire ahead of the 2026 season. She broke into the WNBA in 2022 with the Atlanta Dream.

items.headline: Won 2021 NCAA championship with Stanford; named Final Four MVP
items.summary: Haley Jones won an NCAA national championship with Stanford in 2021 and was named the Final Four Most Outstanding Player. She was a standout for the Cardinal before being drafted in the 2023 WNBA Draft.

items.headline: Selected 15th by Portland Fire in 2026 expansion draft
items.summary: Haley Jones was selected 15th overall by the Portland Fire in the 2026 WNBA expansion draft on April 3, 2026. She had been playing for the Dallas Wings on a hardship contract and earned a rest-of-season deal in July 2025.

items.headline: Holds UConn career assist record; two-time Big East Defensive POY
items.summary: Nika Muhl, who played for perennial national powerhouse UConn, holds the program's career assist record. She was named Big East Defensive Player of the Year twice during her college career.

items.headline: Sidelined with second consecutive ACL tear; misses 2026 WNBA season
items.summary: Nika Muhl will miss the 2026 WNBA season after suffering a right ACL tear while playing for Croatia in Eurobasket Qualifiers. This marks the second consecutive season-ending ACL tear for Muhl, as she missed the entire 2025 season after tearing her left ACL while playing for Turkish club Besiktas.

items.headline: Croatian guard from Zagreb; represented Croatia internationally
items.summary: Nika Muhl is from Zagreb, Croatia and has represented the Croatian national team in international competition, including Eurobasket Qualifiers. She brings international experience to the Portland Fire.

items.headline: Affair with Celtics staffer ended 13-year relationship with Nia Long
items.summary: In 2022, Boston Celtics head coach Ime Udoka had an inappropriate and consensual relationship with a Celtics team employee (Kathleen Lynch), which led to his one-season suspension. The scandal devastated his 13-year partner, actress Nia Long, who had just moved her family to Boston two weeks prior for his job. Long later revealed the staffer's identity publicly and spoke about her heartbreak, particularly concerning her son's reaction.

items.headline: Jumped from podcast host to Lakers head coach with zero prior coaching experience
items.summary: In June 2024, JJ Redick was hired as the Lakers' 29th head coach after building massive influence through his podcast 'The Old Man and the Three' (320M+ YouTube views). He had no prior NBA coaching experience aside from volunteering as a fourth-grade basketball coach. Upon taking the job, he ended his podcasting career to focus fully on coaching, making this one of the most unconventional coaching hires in recent NBA history.

items.headline: Iconic 2001 Finals stepover by AI became defining moment of Lue's legacy
items.summary: During Game 1 of the 2001 NBA Finals, Allen Iverson crossed over and stepped over a fallen Tyronn Lue after hitting a fadeaway jumper. Though Lue played elite defense through regulation (holding AI to 3 points), Iverson dominated in overtime, with the stepover becoming an immortalized image in basketball history. Decades later, they transformed their on-court rivalry into genuine friendship.

items.headline: Legendary 'Heat Culture' mythology built and sustained by Pat Riley since 1995
items.summary: Pat Riley, known as 'The Godfather' of the Miami Heat, has built a powerhouse organization around the concept of Heat Culture since becoming team president in 1995. The culture emphasizes extreme discipline, the thrill of victory, and next-man-up mentality that democratizes depth. However, some observers now suggest that Heat Culture's aura no longer carries the same magnetism with modern players, and Riley's methods may be outdated in today's era.

items.headline: Home in Coral Gables heavily damaged by fire in November 2025
items.summary: On November 6, 2025, Miami Heat head coach Erik Spoelstra's residence in Coral Gables, Florida was heavily damaged in a fire while he was away with the team on a chartered flight back to Miami. The incident garnered community support as Spoelstra has been a prominent civic presence in Miami.

items.headline: Found out about Luka Doncic trade to Lakers at the '11th hour'
items.summary: In 2025, Jason Kidd was blindsided when the Dallas Mavericks traded franchise star Luka Doncic to the Los Angeles Lakers. Kidd revealed he learned of the blockbuster trade only moments before it became public, describing the timing as the '11th hour.' The shocking move came 14 months after Kidd had signed a multi-year contract extension in May 2024.

items.headline: Fired after one season despite $50M, five-year deal; $40M still owed
items.summary: In May 2024, Mike Budenholzer signed a five-year, $50 million deal to coach the Phoenix Suns. After going 36-46 and missing the playoffs in his only season, the Suns fired him on April 14, 2025. Reports indicate Budenholzer had a contentious relationship with Devin Booker and struggled to manage the locker room. Phoenix owes him approximately $40 million of the remaining contract, while also owing Frank Vogel millions from his prior firing.

items.headline: Arrested by FBI in October 2025 on wire fraud and money laundering charges
items.summary: On October 23, 2025, just hours after the Trail Blazers' season opener, the FBI arrested Portland head coach Chauncey Billups as part of a sprawling gambling investigation. Billups is charged with money laundering conspiracy and wire fraud conspiracy, each carrying up to 20 years prison. Prosecutors allege he worked with scammers to attract unsuspecting gamblers to rigged games backed by the Mafia and tipped off co-conspirators about Blazers' internal decisions to gain betting advantages.

items.headline: Hired by Hawks in February 2023 after Nate McMillan firing
items.summary: On February 26, 2023, Quin Snyder was hired as the Atlanta Hawks' head coach on a five-year contract, just five days after the team fired Nate McMillan. Snyder immediately began implementing his program and standards to make a playoff push and evaluate the franchise's roster heading into the offseason, demonstrating the organization's commitment to quick turnaround.

items.headline: Moved from Kings to Knicks after unanimous Coach of the Year award
items.summary: Mike Brown was fired by the Sacramento Kings on December 27, 2024, after a 13-18 start, despite winning the 2022-23 NBA Coach of the Year award by unanimous vote (100/100 votes)—the first coach to achieve this honor. In 2025, he was hired by the New York Knicks on a four-year, $40 million deal. His departure marked the end of Sacramento's playoff renaissance after a 17-year drought.

items.headline: Promoted from interim to permanent Kings head coach with three-year deal
items.summary: Doug Christie took over as interim coach of the Sacramento Kings in December 2024 when Mike Brown was fired. On May 1, 2025, he was promoted to permanent head coach and signed a three-year deal with two guaranteed seasons. As of 2026, Christie was retained for the 2026-27 season despite a disappointing 22-59 finish, with the Kings believing they couldn't properly evaluate his coaching during a year of significant injuries.

items.headline: Fired by Pelicans after 2-10 start and 150-190 record over four-plus seasons
items.summary: Willie Green was fired as head coach of the New Orleans Pelicans on November 15, 2025, after a disappointing 2-10 start to the season. In over four seasons with the team, Green compiled a 150-190 record and made the playoffs twice (losing first-round games to Phoenix in 2022 and Oklahoma City in 2024). James Borrego was named interim replacement after Green's departure.

items.title: Son of two-time Slam Dunk champion Jason Richardson
items.summary: Jase (Jason Anthoney Richardson II) is the son of Warriors legend Jason Richardson, who won the NBA Dunk Contest in 2002 and 2003. Following in his father's footsteps, Jase was selected 25th overall by Orlando in the 2025 NBA Draft.
items.headline: Son of two-time Slam Dunk champion Jason Richardson

items.title: Competing in 2026 Slam Dunk Contest with father as mentor
items.summary: Jase was selected for the 2026 AT&T Slam Dunk Contest on Feb 14, with his father Jason Richardson serving as his personal coach and mentor. Jase is attempting to become the first second-generation Slam Dunk Contest champion.
items.headline: Competing in 2026 Slam Dunk Contest with father as mentor

items.title: Sister Gabriela drafted WNBA top 5 — historic Mexican-American sibling duo
items.summary: Jaime's younger sister Gabriela was drafted 5th overall by the Chicago Sky in the 2026 WNBA draft, making them the first Mexican-American siblings to be drafted by both NBA and WNBA. Jaime flew to Phoenix to watch her win the UCLA national championship just days before her draft selection.
items.headline: Sister Gabriela drafted WNBA top 5 — historic Mexican-American sibling duo

items.title: Three-generation basketball family from Mexico + California roots
items.summary: Jaime comes from a three-generation basketball legacy. His parents, Angela (Norwegian) and Jaime Sr., both played college basketball at Concordia University Irvine where they met. The family has strong ties to Zapotlanejo, Mexico, where Jaime's grandmother's extended family lives.
items.headline: Three-generation basketball family from Mexico + California roots
McCarthy frustrated with Jones' hands-on management
items.description: Reports indicated McCarthy grew 'fed up' with owner Jerry Jones' constant meddling and undermining of coaching decisions, with insiders saying McCarthy did his best despite interference from the owner.
items.summary: Reports indicated McCarthy grew 'fed up' with owner Jerry Jones' constant meddling and undermining of coaching decisions, with insiders saying McCarthy did his best despite interference from the owner.

items.headline: Coaching dynasty: son of legendary Mike Shanahan
items.description: Kyle Shanahan is the son of Mike Shanahan, the legendary Denver Broncos HC (2x Super Bowl champion). Father-son coaching dynamics add complexity to Kyle's leadership at SF, with the legacy of his father's success as a backdrop.
items.summary: Kyle Shanahan is the son of Mike Shanahan, the legendary Denver Broncos HC (2x Super Bowl champion). Father-son coaching dynamics add complexity to Kyle's leadership at SF, with the legacy of his father's success as a backdrop.

items.headline: Overcame addiction and single-parent upbringing
items.description: McDaniel's father died in a car crash when he was four years old. His mother Donna raised him as a single parent, working multiple jobs while struggling financially. McDaniel said he had no idea they were poor as a kid, thanking his mother for her sacrifices. He got sober on January 4, 2016 after struggling with alcohol.

McDaniel said he had no idea they were poor as a kid, thanking his mother for her sacrifices. He got sober on January 4, 2016 after struggling with alcohol.

items.headline: Fired from Texans for unreliability
items.description: McDaniel was let go from Houston Texans due to unreliability stemming from his struggles with alcohol before getting sober. This stint taught him lessons about maturity and accountability.
items.summary: McDaniel was let go from Houston Texans due to unreliability stemming from his struggles with alcohol before getting sober. This stint taught him lessons about maturity and accountability.

items.headline: Dianna Russini affair scandal rocks Patriots
items.description: In April 2026, photos emerged of Vrabel and ESPN/Athletic reporter Dianna Russini holding hands, hugging poolside, in a hot tub, and on a rooftop at a luxury Sedona hotel. Both were married to other people. The affair is alleged to have roots back to 2020 when Russini covered Vrabel's Tennessee Titans for ESPN.

Both were married to other people. The affair is alleged to have roots back to 2020 when Russini covered Vrabel's Tennessee Titans for ESPN.

items.headline: Russini resigns from The Athletic; Vrabel seeks counseling
items.description: Following the scandal, Dianna Russini resigned from The Athletic after a week. Vrabel left the Patriots to seek counseling and missed three days of the 2026 NFL Draft. Additional photos from March 2020 showed the two 'all over each other' at a NYC bar during Vrabel's Titans tenure.

items.headline: Fined $100k for medical tent violation; fired after 2-8 start
items.description: Daboll was fined $100,000 for approaching the medical tent while Jaxson Dart was being examined for a concussion. GM Joe Schoen even listened in on headsets during games to monitor communication. Fired after 2-8 start in 2025, finishing 20-40-1 regular season record.

items.headline: Son-in-law of former Packers head coach Mike Sherman
items.description: Zac Taylor married Sarah, daughter of former Green Bay Packers and Texas A&M head coach Mike Sherman. Taylor began his coaching career under his father-in-law at Texas A&M in 2008 as tight ends coach and graduate assistant.
items.summary: Zac Taylor married Sarah, daughter of former Green Bay Packers and Texas A&M head coach Mike Sherman. Taylor began his coaching career under his father-in-law at Texas A&M in 2008 as tight ends coach and graduate assistant.

items.headline: Returning Jets legend becomes third Black HC in franchise history
items.description: Aaron Glenn was hired as NY Jets head coach on January 22, 2025. Glenn was a Jets first-round pick (12th overall) in 1994 and played his first eight seasons with the team, making an emotional return to his former franchise as head coach.
items.summary: Aaron Glenn was hired as NY Jets head coach on January 22, 2025. Glenn was a Jets first-round pick (12th overall) in 1994 and played his first eight seasons with the team, making an emotional return to his former franchise as head coach.

items.headline: Former Lions defensive coordinator brings elite defense pedigree
items.description: Glenn served as Detroit Lions defensive coordinator from 2021-2024, helping build one of the league's top defenses. Now brings defensive expertise to Jets after strong tenure in Detroit.
items.summary: Glenn served as Detroit Lions defensive coordinator from 2021-2024, helping build one of the league's top defenses. Now brings defensive expertise to Jets after strong tenure in Detroit.

items.headline: Co-authored faith-based book about infidelity and addiction recovery
items.description: Just 16 months before becoming Panthers HC, Canales and wife Lizzy co-authored 'This Marriage? The Question That Changed Everything,' detailing his recovery from infidelity, pornography addiction, and binge drinking. Book describes 'dark confessions' and how he cheated on Lizzy one month before marriage.

items.headline: Panthers knew of book before hiring; now sober and reconciled
items.description: The Panthers organization was aware of the book before hiring Canales. Canales states he has overcome infidelity and pornography issues with counseling help and has stopped drinking completely. He and his wife have four children and have rebuilt a healthy Christian-based marriage.

items.headline: Cardinals tampering scandal and early dismissal
items.description: Two days after losing Super Bowl LVII with Eagles, Gannon was hired by Cardinals (Feb 14, 2023). But April 27, 2023 revealed Gannon had impermissibly interviewed with Cards the week after NFC Championship. NFL penalized both teams—swapped 3rd-round picks.
items.summary: Two days after losing Super Bowl LVII with Eagles, Gannon was hired by Cardinals (Feb 14, 2023). But April 27, 2023 revealed Gannon had impermissibly interviewed with Cards the week after NFC Championship. NFL penalized both teams—swapped 3rd-round picks.

items.headline: Fired after 3 losing seasons; 3-14 record in final year
items.description: Gannon's Cardinals never found success, finishing 3-14 in 2025 and last in NFC West. He was fired on January 5, 2026, after just three seasons. The quick exit marked a stunning fall from his Eagles Super Bowl appearance.
items.summary: Gannon's Cardinals never found success, finishing 3-14 in 2025 and last in NFC West. He was fired on January 5, 2026, after just three seasons. The quick exit marked a stunning fall from his Eagles Super Bowl appearance.

items.headline: First Black full-time head coach in Falcons franchise history
items.description: Raheem Morris became the first Black full-time head coach in Atlanta Falcons history when hired as HC in January 2024. Morris had previous experience with the franchise as wide receivers coach (2015-2019) and interim HC (2020).
items.summary: Raheem Morris became the first Black full-time head coach in Atlanta Falcons history when hired as HC in January 2024. Morris had previous experience with the franchise as wide receivers coach (2015-2019) and interim HC (2020).

items.headline: Two-time Super Bowl champion but fired after disappointing tenure
items.description: Morris won two Super Bowls as DC (2002 Buccaneers, 2022 Rams) but struggled as Falcons HC, going 8-9 in 2025 and missing playoffs. He and GM Terry Fontenot were fired on January 4, 2026, ending a disappointing two-year run.
items.summary: Morris won two Super Bowls as DC (2002 Buccaneers, 2022 Rams) but struggled as Falcons HC, going 8-9 in 2025 and missing playoffs. He and GM Terry Fontenot were fired on January 4, 2026, ending a disappointing two-year run.

items.headline: Youngest HC at hire; meteoric rise from Ravens coaching intern
items.description: Macdonald began as a Ravens defensive coaching intern in 2014. Through hard work and defensive innovation, he rose from quality control to linebackers coach, then defensive coordinator. Hired by Seahawks on January 31, 2024, he was the youngest HC in the NFL at that time.
items.summary: Macdonald began as a Ravens defensive coaching intern in 2014. Through hard work and defensive innovation, he rose from quality control to linebackers coach, then defensive coordinator. Hired by Seahawks on January 31, 2024, he was the youngest HC in the NFL at that time.

items.headline: Georgia grad and finance whiz with master's in sports management
items.description: Macdonald graduated summa cum laude from Georgia Terry College of Business (finance degree) and earned master's in sports management from Georgia. His analytical approach earned him a 'genius' moniker in coaching circles for innovative defensive schemes.
items.summary: Macdonald graduated summa cum laude from Georgia Terry College of Business (finance degree) and earned master's in sports management from Georgia. His analytical approach earned him a 'genius' moniker in coaching circles for innovative defensive schemes.

items.headline: Super Bowl XLII champion becomes Raiders HC, quickly fired
items.description: Pierce was a member of the NY Giants' Super Bowl XLII championship team (2008) and Pro Bowl player in 2006. After playing career ended, he coached high school and college before being hired by Raiders as HC on January 19, 2024, following interim role.
items.summary: Pierce was a member of the NY Giants' Super Bowl XLII championship team (2008) and Pro Bowl player in 2006. After playing career ended, he coached high school and college before being hired by Raiders as HC on January 19, 2024, following interim role.

items.headline: 4-13 record; fired after single full season
items.description: Pierce's Raiders had been 0-2 when he took over as interim. In his only full season (2025), the team went 4-13 and finished dead last in AFC West. He was fired on January 7, 2025, ending his brief Raiders tenure.
items.summary: Pierce's Raiders had been 0-2 when he took over as interim. In his only full season (2025), the team went 4-13 and finished dead last in AFC West. He was fired on January 7, 2025, ending his brief Raiders tenure.

items.headline: Emotional coach captured crying during national anthem
items.description: During a broadcast of Eagles game, cameras caught tears streaming down Sirianni's face as Chris Stapleton performed the national anthem. The emotional moment showed Sirianni the man behind the loud football coach persona, demonstrating his deep investment in the game.

items.headline: Quarterback developer from Rivers to Hurts in NFL
items.description: Steichen earned his degree in journalism and media studies from UNLV and played QB there (2003-2006). He became known for his expertise in developing young QBs, having worked with Philip Rivers (Chargers), Justin Herbert (Chargers), and Jalen Hurts (Eagles).
items.summary: Steichen earned his degree in journalism and media studies from UNLV and played QB there (2003-2006). He became known for his expertise in developing young QBs, having worked with Philip Rivers (Chargers), Justin Herbert (Chargers), and Jalen Hurts (Eagles).

items.headline: Eagles OC becomes Colts HC; retained for 2026
items.description: Steichen was the Philadelphia Eagles' offensive coordinator (2021-2022), contributing to multiple Pro Bowl selections and Super Bowl appearances. Hired by the Indianapolis Colts as HC on February 14, 2023. After finishing the 2025 season, he was retained as Colts HC for 2026.
items.summary: Steichen was the Philadelphia Eagles' offensive coordinator (2021-2022), contributing to multiple Pro Bowl selections and Super Bowl appearances. Hired by the Indianapolis Colts as HC on February 14, 2023. After finishing the 2025 season, he was retained as Colts HC for 2026.

items.headline: Brother Mike LaFleur becomes Arizona Cardinals HC in 2025
items.description: Matt LaFleur is one of three brotherly coaching duos to simultaneously coach in the NFL. His younger brother Mike became the Arizona Cardinals head coach, making them one of two Harbaugh-like sibling pair leading NFL franchises at the same time.
items.summary: Matt LaFleur is one of three brotherly coaching duos to simultaneously coach in the NFL. His younger brother Mike became the Arizona Cardinals head coach, making them one of two Harbaugh-like sibling pair leading NFL franchises at the same time.

items.headline: Packers HC since 2019; son of ex-linebacker Denny LaFleur
items.description: LaFleur was born to Denny and Kristi LaFleur in Mount Pleasant, Michigan. His father Denny was a linebacker at Central Michigan who won a national championship in 1974. Matt played QB for Saginaw Valley State before brief professional stint in Indoor Football League before committing to coaching.

Matt played QB for Saginaw Valley State before brief professional stint in Indoor Football League before committing to coaching.

items.headline: Super Bowl LI collapse in Atlanta; redemption with Commanders
items.description: Quinn was Atlanta Falcons HC from 2015-2020. In Super Bowl LI (2017 season), his Falcons led the Patriots 28-3 with 8:31 left in Q3 before losing 34-28 in OT—the largest Super Bowl comeback ever. This haunted him for years.
items.summary: Quinn was Atlanta Falcons HC from 2015-2020. In Super Bowl LI (2017 season), his Falcons led the Patriots 28-3 with 8:31 left in Q3 before losing 34-28 in OT—the largest Super Bowl comeback ever. This haunted him for years.

items.headline: Returns to head coaching after 4-year hiatus; rebuilds Commanders
items.description: Quinn was hired as Washington Commanders HC on February 3, 2024, after serving as defensive coordinator for Dallas Cowboys. His return to head coaching represents a redemptive second chance 8 years after the Super Bowl LI trauma. Commanders became contenders under his leadership.
